title: 27 Tiqimt — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly
narrative:
Tiqimt

The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Crucifixion

St. Nestor of Thessalonica, Martyr

St. Capitolina and Eroteis, Martyrs

On the twenty-seventh of Tiqimt, the monthly Crucifixion commemoration arrives in its second appearance of the new year's cycle — and in the month of interiority, the Cross is contemplated most naturally in its interior form: the cross that is carried within, the dying of the self that the interior life requires as its most fundamental and most continuous practice. The Cross of Maskal blazed on the hilltops; the monthly Cross of the twenty-seventh speaks in quieter tones, asking the community not about the triumph they have celebrated but about the dying they are practicing. 'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily.' The word is 'daily' — not once, not annually in the Maskal celebration, but in the ordinary recurring rhythm of the second month's quiet days. The monthly Crucifixion is the calendar's insistence on this daily dimension of the Cross's reality.

St. Nestor of Thessalonica was the young man whom St. Demetrius blessed before he entered the arena to fight the gladiator Lyaeus — the friend whose courage Demetrius sustained at the cost of his own life, and who won the fight and was subsequently executed for his victory. Nestor's story is inseparable from Demetrius's, and together they present the interior life's most searching question about the relationship between the support received and the cost borne. Demetrius gave everything to sustain Nestor's one moment of courage; Nestor's courage was real — he entered the arena — but it was not self-generated. On the day of the monthly Crucifixion, Nestor and Demetrius together are the testimony that the Cross is always born in community: there is always the one who blesses and the one who receives the blessing, and the one who blesses often bears the cost that the blessed one does not.

Sts. Capitolina and Eroteis were a mistress and her servant-girl who were martyred together in Cappadocia — Capitolina a wealthy noblewoman, Eroteis a slave. Their joint martyrdom is one of the calendar's most pointed testimonies about the Cross's social subversion: the Cross, when genuinely carried, dissolves the distinctions between mistress and slave, between the wealthy and the owned, between the one who commands and the one who serves. They died together, were crowned together, and are commemorated together — the noblewoman and her servant, made equal in the blood of the same Cross. On the twenty-seventh of Tiqimt, in the month of the Covenant of Mercy, Capitolina and Eroteis stand as the testimony that the mercy which the month honors is a leveling mercy — not the mercy that makes distinctions more tolerable but the mercy that makes them irrelevant.

The monthly Crucifixion, Nestor, and Capitolina and Eroteis together on the twenty-seventh day of Tiqimt present the Cross in its most searching interior and social dimensions. The Cross is daily; it is borne in community; it levels every distinction that the world considers fundamental. The interior life that Tiqimt has been cultivating across its twenty-seven days arrives at this point — the monthly Cross — as the place where everything the month has offered is tested and confirmed: the steadfastness of the martyrs, the quiet of the cell, the sustained fidelity of the desert fathers, the practical mercy of the seamstress, all of it held together in the one act of the daily dying that the Cross requires and the Covenant of Mercy makes possible.
